questions.text: Who among the following described the Indian Constitution as a 'quasi-federal' state?
questions.explanation: Constitutional expert K.C. Wheare described India's federal structure as 'quasi-federal' — an intermediate form between unitary and federal.
questions.source: Confirmed recurring SSC CGL question format

questions.text:
Assertion (A): Indian federalism is called 'Quasi-federal'.
Reason (R): India has an independent judiciary with the power of Judicial Review.
Which is correct?
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both statements are independently true, but judicial review is not what makes India 'quasi-federal' — that comes from the strong-Centre bias (e.g. Emergency provisions), not judicial review.
questions.source: Confirmed recurring SSC CGL Assertion-Reason question format

questions.text: The Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) were borrowed from the Constitution of which country?
questions.explanation: DPSP, along with the method of Presidential election and nomination of Rajya Sabha members, were borrowed from Ireland's Constitution (1937).
questions.source: Confirmed recurring SSC CGL question format

questions.text: Which Article of the Constitution lays down the procedure for its own amendment?
questions.explanation: Article 368 lays down the three-tier amendment procedure (ordinary law / special majority / special majority + state ratification).
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated based on commonly confused Article-number clustering (352/356/360/368)

questions.text: Which Article declares India to be a 'Union of States' rather than a 'Federation of States'?
questions.explanation: Article 1 declares 'India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States' — a deliberate phrasing choice reflecting India's quasi-federal character.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated based on a frequently tested opening-Article fact

questions.text: By which Constitutional Amendment was the voting age in India reduced from 21 to 18 years?
questions.explanation: The 61st Amendment Act, 1988 lowered the voting age from 21 to 18 — a fact often missed since students assume 18 was always the rule.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — an underused but exam-relevant fact

questions.text: Local self-government (Panchayats and Municipalities) was added as a formal third tier of government by which Amendments?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment (Panchayats) and 74th Amendment (Municipalities), both in 1992, created the third tier of government.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — frequently underrated despite being a high-yield static fact

questions.text: How many Fundamental Duties were originally added to the Constitution by the 42nd Amendment, 1976?
questions.explanation: 10 Fundamental Duties were added in 1976; an 11th (regarding parents/guardians providing education to children aged 6-14) was added later by the 86th Amendment, 2002.
questions.source: Pattern frequently recurring across SSC CGL/CHSL polity papers in various phrasings

questions.text: Match the type of Emergency to its correct Article number: National Emergency, President's Rule, Financial Emergency.
questions.explanation: National Emergency — Article 352; President's Rule (State Emergency) — Article 356; Financial Emergency — Article 360. The numbers climb by 4 in this exact order.
questions.source: Pattern frequently recurring — Emergency Article-number matching is a classic SSC CGL question style
